coredata.dc:title: Simple and facile carbon dots based electrochemical biosensor for TNF-α targeting in cancer patient's sample
coredata.dc:creator: Sri, Smriti
Lakshmi, G.B.V.S.
Gulati, Payal
Chauhan, Deepika
Thakkar, Alok
Solanki, Pratima R.
coredata.dc:description: Tumour Necrosis Factor (TNF-α) is a pro-inflammatory cytokine having key roles in cell death, differentiation, survival, proliferation, migration and is a modulator of immune system. Therefore, TNF-α is an ideal biomarker for several disease diagnosis including cancer. However, out of all the biomarkers of cancer, TNF-α) is less explored for cancer detection. Only a few reports are available of developing biosensors for TNF-α targeting in human serum samples. Also, Carbon Dots (CDs) remains less explored in biosensor application. In this regard, for the first time, a sensitive and low-cost electrochemical biosensor based on CDs has developed. CDs were synthesized by simple yet facile microwave pyrolysis. Poly methyl methacrylate (PMMA) was selected as the matrix to hold CDs to fabricate the biosensing platform. This novel CD-PMMA nanocomposite featuring excellent biocompatibility, exceptional electrocatalytic conductivity, and large surface area. CD-PMMA was applied as transducing material to efficiently conjugate antibodies specific towards TNF-α and fabricate electrochemical immunosensor for specific detection of TNF-α. The fabricated immunosensor was used for the detection of TNF-α within a wide dynamic range of 0.05–160 pg mL−1 with a lower detection limit of 0.05 pg mL−1 and sensitivity of 5.56 pg mL−1 cm−2. Furthermore, this CDs based immunosensor retains high sensitivity, selectivity, and stability. This immunosensor demonstrated a high correlation with the conventional technique, Enzyme-Linked Immunosorbent Assay for early screening of cancer patient serum samples.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
PMMA
Composite matrix
TNF-α
Cancer diagnosis
Electrochemical biosensor

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of CDs and CD-PMMA solution
Cancer patient's serum samples preparation and ELISA assay
Interference, reproducibility, repeatability, and stability studies

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Department of Biotechnology, Ministry of Science and Technology, India
Department of Science and Technology, Ministry of Science and Technology, India

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: Authors, Smriti Sri and Deepika Chauhan are thankful to UGC, New Delhi, India for providing fellowship in the form of SRF. GBVSL is thankful to DST, India funding through the women scientist project. PRS is thankful to DST, India for funding. This work was financially supported by the Department of Biotechnology (DBT) ; Indo-Russia project (DBT/IC-2/Indo-Russia/2017-19/02), ICMR project, and Department of Science and Technology (DST) ; BDTD project ( TDP/BDTD/24/2019/General ), Government of India .

Authors, Smriti Sri and Deepika Chauhan are thankful to UGC, New Delhi, India for providing fellowship in the form of SRF. GBVSL is thankful to DST, India funding through the women scientist project. PRS is thankful to DST, India for funding. This work was financially supported by the Department of Biotechnology (DBT); Indo-Russia project (DBT/IC-2/Indo-Russia/2017-19/02), ICMR project, and Department of Science and Technology (DST); BDTD project (TDP/BDTD/24/2019/General), Government of India.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic representation of the stepwise assembly of the fabricated immunosensor.
(a) TEM images of CD (b) size distribution of CD.
FTIR spectra of (a) CD-PMMA/ITO, (b) anti-TNF-α/CD-PMMA/ITO, and (c) ΒSA/anti-TNF-α/CD-PMMA/ITO.

(a) pH study of immunoelectrode, (b) DPV comparison of (i) CD-PMMA/ITO electrode (ii) anti-TNF-α/CD-PMMA/ITO and (iii) BSA/anti-TNF-α/CD-PMMA/ITO immunoelectrode, (c) and (d) Scan rate study of CD-PMMA/ITO electrode and BSA/anti-TNF-α/CD-PMMA/ITO immunoelectrode at different scan rates (10–100 mV s−1); inset of (c) and (d) shows plot between peak current and the square root of scan rate of respective electrodes in 0.2 M PBS having ferri/ferro as redox coupler.

(a) Amperometric response of the immunosensor to different concentration of TNF-α, from 0.05 to 160 pg mL−1, (b) Calibration curve of immunosensor at different concentrations of TNF-α, (c) Comparative analysis of peak current of serum and standard samples using fabricated immunoelectrode, and (d) DPV response of cancerous patient samples.
Proposed mechanism of sensing of TNF-α.
(a) Interference, (b) reproducibility, (c) Repeatability, and (d) shelf-life studies of BSA/anti-TNF-α/CD-PMMA/ITO immunoelectrode.
Summary of few biosensors developed for TNF-α detection

row.entry:
Magnetic Microparticles onto Screen-Printed
Au Electrode
(ii) Dual screen-printed electrodes modified with functionalized double walled carbon nanotubes
Rat blood serum infected with Pb18
AuNPs/S-MWCNTs as sensor platform and bimetallic Ni/Cu-MOFs as sensor amplification

head.ce:title: Simple and facile carbon dots based electrochemical biosensor for TNF-α targeting in cancer patient's sample

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Special Centre for Nanoscience, Jawaharlal Nehru University, New Delhi, 110067, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Special Centre for Nanoscience, Jawaharlal Nehru University, New Delhi, India, 110067

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: All India Institute of Medical Sciences, Ansari Nagar, New Delhi, 110029, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: All India Institute of Medical Sciences

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: All India Institute of Medical Sciences, Ansari Nagar, New Delhi, India, 110029

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Tumour Necrosis Factor (TNF-α) is a pro-inflammatory cytokine having key roles in cell death, differentiation, survival, proliferation, migration and is a modulator of immune system. Therefore, TNF-α is an ideal biomarker for several disease diagnosis including cancer. However, out of all the biomarkers of cancer, TNF-α) is less explored for cancer detection. Only a few reports are available of developing biosensors for TNF-α targeting in human serum samples. Also, Carbon Dots (CDs) remains less explored in biosensor application. In this regard, for the first time, a sensitive and low-cost electrochemical biosensor based on CDs has developed. CDs were synthesized by simple yet facile microwave pyrolysis. Poly methyl methacrylate (PMMA) was selected as the matrix to hold CDs to fabricate the biosensing platform. This novel CD-PMMA nanocomposite featuring excellent biocompatibility, exceptional electrocatalytic conductivity, and large surface area. CD-PMMA was applied as transducing material to efficiently conjugate antibodies specific towards TNF-α and fabricate electrochemical immunosensor for specific detection of TNF-α. The fabricated immunosensor was used for the detection of TNF-α within a wide dynamic range of 0.05–160 pg mL−1 with a lower detection limit of 0.05 pg mL−1 and sensitivity of 5.56 pg mL−1 cm−2. Furthermore, this CDs based immunosensor retains high sensitivity, selectivity, and stability. This immunosensor demonstrated a high correlation with the conventional technique, Enzyme-Linked Immunosorbent Assay for early screening of cancer patient serum samples.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon Dots (CDs) have garnered enormous interest to the scientific community. It shows brilliant photoluminescence, electronic conductivity, high biocompatibility, excellent solubility, and resistance to photobleaching [1]. Apart from that, CDs can be very easily synthesized from several chemical or green precursors [2,3], and modified very fast and effortlessly. These CDs are well studied in bioimaging, drug delivery, optical sensors, electrochemiluminescence, photocatalysis due to their non-toxic nature [3,4]. The fluorescence of CDs can be tuned from visible to NIR range by carefully selecting the carbon precursor, synthesis method, and heteroatom doping [5]. Additionally, CDs are rapidly cleared from the body and not metabolized by the cells [4]. Consequently, CDs are much sought after nanomaterials for biosensing and the research of CDs in biosensing has advanced significantly in consequent years. The chemical inertness of CDs is also desired for nanoprobes in biosensing [4,6].

CDs have a very popular cousin named graphene quantum dots (GQDs) and both are often confused with each other. CDs or carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are carbon nanoparticles usually less than 10 nm in size, shows brilliant fluorescence and biocompatibility. However, graphene quantum dots (GQDs) have the structure of graphene lattice (thickness less than 2 nm) with crystalline morphology. CDs and GQDs are often confused with each other because of their similar optical properties. However, there are a lot structural difference between them. CDs can be synthesized via different top-down or bottom-up synthesis process from organic or inorganic starting materials. While, GQDs are either synthesized through nanolithography or by chemical breakdown of GO. The optical properties of CDs and GQDs may look same but have different origins. CDs fluorescence arise from either conjugated π-domain or through surface defects form surface passivation. While, the fluorescence in GQDs is caused by defects or isolated π-domain of graphene sheets. The fluorescence emission of CDs can be tuned via surface groups rather than size. Therefore, the CDs PL colour is not efficiently controlled by size modification. However, in GQDs the fluorescence is considered the transition from the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) to the highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO). The bandgap of HOMO-LUMO depends on the GQDs size and decreases as the GQDs size increases. Therefore, different mixtures of GQDs with different sizes, have different fluorescence [7,8]. In this way the increase in size of the GQDs (decrease in energy gap) shifts the colour of the GQDs emission form blue to brown [3,8,9].
GQDs possess the characteristics of both CDs and graphene. For that reason, GQDs have been explored more than CDs in electrochemical biosensing. However, the CDs remain somewhat unexplored.

There are only a few reports of CDs being used in the development of biosensing platforms for glucose [10,11], 2,4,6-trinitrotoluene [12], dopamine [13], patulin, O2 [14], and H2O2 reduction [15]. Our group has recently developed an electrochemical biosensor for the detection of Vitamin D2 based on CDs [16]. Presently there is no report of using CDs as a biosensor for early cancer detection. In literature, there is only one report available on the fabrication of an electrochemical aptasensor based on GQDs for early cancer detection. Huang et al., developed the high sensitive aptasensor for carcinoembryonic antigen (CEA) detection based on Pb2+ dependent DNAzyme-assisted signal amplification on GQDs-ionic liquid-nafion (GQDs-IL-NFs) [17]. In this respect, this is the first report on CD based biosensing platform for early cancer detection in patient's serum samples.

Matrix is an important component of a biosensor. Function-wise, a matrix comprises of a detecting layer of immobilized materials (antibody and antigen). So, it is one of the most crucial parts of the biosensor fabrication step. Since CDs are highly hydrophilic in nature, this made it extremely difficult for electrode fabrication. Also, CDs cannot be electrophoretically deposited onto the electrode this further limited our fabrication step. Therefore, a proper matrix is the need of the hour. Bhatnagar et al., in their study, explored the role of polyamidoamine (PAMAM) as a matrix for GQDs. Taking that into conscience polymethyl methacrylate (PMMA) was exploited as a matrix for CDs. The results with PMMA were exciting. The role of PMMA as a 3D matrix had explored by Arya et al., [18]. However, for this PMMA need to be laser engraved and plasma-treated [19,20] to create functional groups on the surface for the successful immobilization of biomolecules. Nevertheless, after adding CDs to the PMMA solution there is no need for this extra step as CDs possess many functional groups along with a very high surface area that assists in the biomolecule immobilization.

Cancer remains one of modern society's greatest challenges with reference to deadly diseases. With the advancement of new drugs and massive research in the cancer field, cancer can be cured in patients. However, the basic requirements remain the same for every cancer that is its early detection. Early detection gives room for the medical practitioner to target cancer cells effectively. Traditional methods of cancer detection take a long time, sophisticated instruments, trained staff and also suffer from a poor lower limit of detection. This restricts its use in the early cancer diagnosis of cancer. The early diagnosis still remains a challenge to the medical world. Researchers in the last decade has developed various biomarkers for early cancer detection. A number of cancerous protein biomarkers have been explored for their potential role in early cancer diagnosis such as Carcinoembryonic antigen (CEA), cancer antigens-125 (CA-125), Cytokeratin 19 Fragment (CYFRA-21), Interleukin-8 (IL-8), IL-6, prostate-specific antigen (PSA), human epidermal growth factor receptor (HER) and tumor necrosis factor-α (TNF-α) [21,22]. There is numerous work reported on cancer biomarkers detection, for instance on Interleukins [23–27], CEA [20,28–30], CYFRA-21 [31–33] amongst others. But very few reports are available on the detection of TNF-α as one of the biomarkers of cancer in patient serum samples [34–41].

TNF-α has a substantial role in apoptosis, positive association with the rate of propagation, histological tumor status, and hostile behavior of lymph node metastasis and carcinoma [18]. TNF-α is a pleiotropic cytokine that normally exists as a trimer of 47–55 KDa. These peptides play a key role in encompassing the regulation of immune cells, inducing inflammation, tumorigenesis, viral replication, and cell death [37]. TNF-α can either stimulate tumor development or cell apoptosis when fuelled by tumor promoter chemicals. TNF-α can also act as an inducer of inducible nitric oxide synthase (iNOS), thereby inhibiting DNA repair enzymes and hence increasing mutation, a prerequisite for cancer development. Not only in cancer but TNF-α also play a paramount role in other disease progressions such as neonatal listeriosis, HIV infection, rheumatoid arthritis, septic shock, severe meningococcaemia, and graft rejection. TNF-α concentration is very low in body fluids such as serum. In serum, TNF-α concentration is below 10 pg ml−1 in healthy individuals. The saliva concentration of TNF-α is merely 3 pg ml−1. However, in the case of cancer or autoimmune disease the concentration of TNF-α may increase to as high as 300 pg ml−1 [38,39]. Hence, detection of a tiny amount of TNF-α is crucial for the understanding of tumor biological manners. Table 1 shows the different biosensors developed to date for the detection of TNF-α.

Barhoumi et al., fabricated highly sensitive label based immunosensors for detection of TNF-α in saliva samples by immobilizing anti-TNF-α antibody (Ab-TNF-α) over Au electrodes (WE) via 4-carboxymethylaniline functionalization and by utilizing electrophoretically deposited polymer-coated magnetic microparticles onto gold modified SPE and found detection limit of 1 pg mL−1 and 0.3 pg mL−1, respectively [34]. Pruna et al., reported novel miniaturized POC opto-electrochemical system for TNF-α determination based on aryl diazonium salt modified ITO electrode and sensing was performed in a linear range of 10 pg mL−1 to 100 pg mL−1 [40]. Bellagambi et al., synthesized electrochemical biosensor for TNF-α determination in both artificial and human saliva based on carboxyl diazonium functionalized Au electrodes and found 3.1 pg mL−1 detection limit of the sensor [41]. Aydın et al., presented impedimetric immunosensor for TNF-α detection by utilizing Poly(3-thiophene acetic acid) modified ITO electrode and LOD of 3.7 fg mL−1 was obtained from a linear sensing range of 0.01 pg mL−1 to 2 pg mL−1 [42]. Mahmoodi et al., performed multi-well chip sensing for analysis of cytokines (TNF-α) in serum of mouse and found LOD of 10 ng/L calculated from sensing range of 10–500 ng/L [43]. Elif Burcu Aydın et al. detected TNF-α by utilizing epoxy silane modified ITO-PET electrode and achieved 3.1 fg mL−1 detection limit [44]. Predabon et al., performed this cytokine detection in rat serum samples by SPR biosensor based on nanohole array of gold nanofilm and achieved 17 pg mL−1 detection limit [45]. Similarly, Yola et al., developed voltametric biosensor for cytokine detection using thiol group (on AuNPs) modified MWCNTs for sensing platform and signal was amplified by incorporating bimetallic Ni/Cu-MOF and this sensor showed detection limit of 2.00 fg mL−1 where analysis was done in plasma samples [46].

In this work, a novel electrochemical immunosensing platform for TNF-α detection was developed for the first time based on CD-PMMA/ITO. A thin film of CD-PMMA was fabricated onto indium tin oxide (ITO) and used for the immobilization of antibody specific for TNF-α (anti-TNF-α) for TNF-α detection. The immunosensor was based on the highly reproducible, facile, and one-pot synthesized CD. Under, optimum conditions, TNF-α was detected with the developed immunosensor. The results showed that the present immunosensor has good performance and it could provide a meek and rapid way for point-of-care testing (POC) of TNF-α.

Citric acid, l-cysteine, Polymethyl methacrylate (PMMA), 1-(3-(dimethylamino)-propyl)-3-ethyl carbodiimide hydrochloride (EDC), and N-hydroxysuccinimide (NHS) were procured from Sigma. Sodium monophosphate (NaH2PO4), sodium diphosphate dihydrate [Na2HPO4 2H2O], sodium chloride (NaCl), potassium ferricyanide (K3[Fe(CN)6]), and potassium ferrocyanide (K4[Fe(CN)6]·3H2O) were obtained from Thermo Fischer. Glacial acetic acid, formic acid, anti- TNF-α and TNF-α protein was purchased from MyBioSource. TNF-α ELISA kit was procured from Elabscience. Phosphate buffer solutions (PBS) at different pH were set using Na2HPO4·2H2O and NaH2PO4 in deionized water with resistivity (18 MΩ cm). Dilutions were organized in PBS at pH 7.4 and kept at 4 °C for further use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of CDs and CD-PMMA solution
ce:section.ce:para: CDs were synthesized using the previously reported method [4] by our group with some modifications. Briefly, 1 g citric acid and 25 mg l-cysteine were liquified in 10 mL of DI water. The solution was pyrolyzed using a microwave. The prepared CDs were then dissolved into 10 mL of 3:1 ratio of glacial acetic acid: formic acid. The colloidal solution was obtained as CDs.

The stepwise procedure of the fabricated immunosensor is shown schematically in Fig. 1 . Briefly, the CD-PMMA solution was prepared by dissolving 2.5% of PMMA in the CD solution obtained above using microwave pyrolysis, slowly with slight heating. The CD-PMMA solution was left for overnight stirring. Consequently, 5 μL of CD-PMMA was drop cast onto hydrolyzed ITO and a thin film formed was then left to air dry overnight. Further, 10 μg mL−1 of monoclonal anti-TNF-α was prepared in PBS for the immobilization antedated by the EDC-NHS (2:1:1 ratio of anti-TNF-α: EDC: NHS) treatment for half an hour at 4 °C. The activated Fc group of anti-TNF-α helps in the covalent bond formation with the electrode surface. The CD-PMMA/ITO and anti-TNF-α were left to attach for 6 h in a humid chamber at 4 °C. After drying and washing BSA solution was used to obliterate the non-specific binding sites between the antigen and electrode surface. After 4 h incubation, BSA/anti-TNF-α/CD-PMMA/ITO was washed with PBS and used for electrochemical studies. The cyclic voltammetry was done in the range of 10 mv/s to 100 v/s scan rate with applied potential of −0.6 to 0.8 V. In the DPV studies the applied potential was same as that of CV i.e. −0.6 to 0.8 V.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cancer patient's serum samples preparation and ELISA assay
ce:section.ce:para: The blood samples of oral cancer patients were collected from the All-India Institute of Medical Sciences (AIIMS), New Delhi. The ethical approval number to use Cancer patients’ serum samples is: IERB Ref. No. 2017/Faculty/140 recommended by Institutional Ethics Review Board, JNU, New Delhi. The serum was separated from the collected blood sample by centrifugation at 3000 rpm for 10 min at 4 °C and used for TNF-α quantification using an ELISA kit. The standard samples were run on an ELISA kit and the level of TNF-α was calorimetrically quantified by the calibration plot of standard samples as mentioned in the kit.

Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) was done on CD-PMMA, anti-TNF-α/CD-PMMA/and BSA/anti-TNF-α/CD-PMMA in the range 500–4000 cm−1 using Perkin–Elmer FT-IR spectrometer, USA. Contact angle studies were performed on PMMA and CD-PMMA by KRÜSS drop shape analyzer. All the electrochemical experiments were performed thrice (n = 3) using 0.2 M PBS solution having ferri/ferro (5 mM) species acting as redox coupler with 0.9% NaCl on AMETEK multichannel instrument. The utilization of a three-electrode system was done where silver-silver chloride (Ag/AgCl), platinum (Pt), and modified ITO coated glass substrate acted as a reference, counter, and working electrode, respectively.

The characterization of CDs was discussed in detail by Sri et al., in the previously published paper [4,47]. Here, TEM images and average size calculations are shown in Fig. 2 . The CDs show n-π∗ transition at 360 nm because of CO bonds. Further, the CDs show excitation independent photoluminescence properties with the maximum excitation at 360 nm and emission in the lower wavelength (blue region) of the visible spectrum. The surface functionalization was confirmed with FTIR studies. The absorption bands in FTIR exhibited that CDs were rich in functional groups containing amine, sulfur, and carboxylic groups and mostly amorphous as revealed by a hump in XRD with slight crystalline behavior as well. Furthermore, the structural fingerprint of CDs was characterized by the presence of the D band (1343 cm−1) and G band (1590 cm−1) in Raman spectroscopy. The main compelling property of CDs is their high quantum yield (QY) that was calculated to be 80%. Additionally, these CDs are zwitterionic in nature with a charge of −0.0147 mV containing three different charge species of −8.06 mV, 35.7 mV, and 146 mV. The zwitterionic nature of CDs imparts an anti-fouling surface emanating minimal non-specific adsorption, thereby prompting an easy bioconjugation with bioanalytes without aggregation.

Fig. 2(a) shows the TEM images of CDs at a magnification of 20 nm. (b) The CDs are well dispersed in nature with size ranging from 1 to 7 nm. The average size of the CD was calculated using image J software and is 4.004 ± 0.767 nm.

The electrode's hydrophilicity and surface wettability rely on its surface chemistry. Since the actual charge resides on the electrode's surface, wettability is very significant. In this respect, CD-PMMA electrodes were studied using contact angle (Supplementry data; Figure S1). The contact angle of 2.5% PMMA was 70° while the contact angle for 2.5% CD-PMMA was 60°. This suggests that the incorporation of CD into PMMA leads to a significant change in hydrophilicity. Further, the zwitterionic nature of CDs makes an anti-foul surface emanating minimal non-specific adsorption, thereby prompting an easy bioconjugation with bioanalytes without aggregation.

The experimental conditions along with the PMMA concentration and pH were optimized for an improved electrocatalytic signal. Further, different concentrations of CD-PMMA were investigated for electrode fabrication such as 5%, 2.5%, and 1%. These were drop cast onto ITO electrodes and the film stability and current were measured. On the 1% CD-PMMA/ITO electrodes, the films were very thin so electrodes study could not be done (Figure S2a). The 5% and 2.5% CD-PMMA/ITO electrodes were quite good and the current value was also significant and stable (Figure S2b). But after immobilization of anti-TNFα onto the 5% CD-PMMA film, it was leached out from the electrodes’ surface. However, 2.5% CD-PMMA/ITO electrodes showed a good response even after anti-TNFα immobilization. After all these optimization studies 2.5% CD-PMMA/ITO electrodes were chosen for further studies.

The pH study was analyzed under the same condition of the CD-PMMA/ITO electrode. As shown in Fig. 4 (a), the peak current increased from pH 6.6 to 7.4 and then decreased with the alteration of pH to 8. This concluded that the electrocatalytic activity of the CD-PMMA/ITO electrode was greatly affected by the pH of the PBS. Concurrently, the higher acidic or alkaline surroundings would influence the proteins’ activity, e.g. antigens and antibodies [48,49].

Fig. 3 shows the FTIR spectra of stepwise modified electrodes. Spectrum (a) shows the FTIR of CD-PMMA/ITO electrode that shows prominent bands at 3525, 2997, 2952, 1720, 1444, 1385, 1244, 1195, 1066, 1147, 987, 960, 840, 752 and 601 cm−1 among the peaks at 2997, 2952, 1720, 1444, 1385, 1244, 1195, 1066, 1147, 987, 840, 752 cm−1 correspond to PMMA [50]. The weak peak at 3525 cm−1 may be ascribed to the –NH stretch of CDs. The two other bands at 2997 cm−1 and 2952 cm−1 were attributed to the C–H stretch. The peak at 1720 cm−1 confirms the existence of the acrylate carboxyl group. The peak at 1440 cm−1 may be assigned to the bending vibrations of the alkyl C–H bond. The peak from 1150 cm−1 to 1250 cm−1 indicates the -C-O-C stretching vibration. The peaks at 987 cm−1, 1066 cm−1, and 840 cm−1 are the hallmark of PMMA [50]. The peak corresponding to –CH out of plane deformation while the peak at 601 cm−1 is credited to SO2 deformation. These results show that CD-PMMA is homogeneously mixed together and imparts the characters of both the CDs and PMMA. Spectrum (b) shows the FTIR of anti-TNFα/CD-PMMA/ITO. The absorption peak at 3525 cm−1 has dwindled when anti-TNFα is immobilized onto the electrode surface. The peak at 2952 cm−1 shifts slightly to 2946 cm−1. Further, there is no change in the peaks at 1720 cm−1, which is characteristic of PMMA. However, the peaks at 1440, 1150, 750, and 601 cm−1 got diminished after the anti-TNFα immobilization indicating the successful immobilization of the antibody by attaching with the surface functional groups. Further, a new peak appeared at 1039 cm−1, due to –CN stretch of amine groups present in anti-TNFα. So, the amine must have formed amide linkage with the CD. The disappearance of characteristics CD peak at 601 cm−1 and the formation of a new bond at 1039 cm−1 further confirms the successful immobilization of anti-TNFα onto the electrode surface. Spectrum (c) shows the FTIR of BSA/anti-TNFα/CD-PMMA/ITO immunoelectrode. Most of the peaks in the spectra are similar to spectra (b) like 2946, 1720 cm−1. However, there is a reappearance of few peaks from spectra (a) in (c) are 1444, 1150, and 752 cm−1. These peaks correspond to bending vibrations of the C–H bond of the alkyl group, -C-O-C stretching vibration, and –CH out of plane deformation, respectively. These peaks reappeared as BSA is rich in carboxyl and amine groups and it may have bonded with the CD surface through the above bonds [51]. These results show that anti-TNFα and BSA are successfully immobilized onto the CD-PMMA/ITO electrodes.

Fig. 4 (b) shows the distinct electrochemical behaviour of CD-PMMA/ITO, anti-TNF-α/CD-PMMA/ITO, and BSA/anti-TNF-α/CD-PMMA/ITO immunoelectrode. DPV is used for the quantitative determination of the proposed biosensor as it is a differential technique involving the application of amplitude potential pulses upon linear ramp potential and the difference between the current is recorded and displayed. Here, it can be comprehended from DPV response varies for different electrodes. The highest current was observed in CD-PMMA/ITO electrodes followed by anti-TNF-α/CD-PMMA/ITO. The least current value was observed for BSA/anti-TNF-α/CD-PMMA/ITO immunoelectrode. The possible explanation for this could be that CD being rich in electroactive species contributes to the electron transfer process thereby enhancing the magnitude of the current. On the immobilization of anti-TNF-α, the protein molecule binds to the electroactive sites of CD on the CD-PMMA/ITO electrode. On further immobilizing BSA, the other electroactive sites on the CD surface are blocked and since BSA are insulating in nature, this led to a drastic reduction in the current value. Moreover, the current values also depict a successful modification of the immunoelectrode surface with bioactive molecules (anti-TNF-α and BSA). The deviation in the peak current of various immunoelectrode is also confirmed by FTIR studies (Fig. 3).

A scan rate study was done on CD-PMMA/ITO electrode and BSA/anti-TNF-α/CD-PMMA/ITO immunoelectrode as a function of scan rate from 10 to 100 mV s−1 to analyse the numerous electrochemical parameters (Fig. 4c and d). The peak value was plotted counter to the root of the scan rate. It was discovered that the anodic (ipa) and cathodic peak currents (ipc) increase linearly with the square root of scan rate (Fig. 4c and d–inset) confirming the diffusion control property of electrochemical reactions. The functionalization of CD-PMMA/ITO with anti-TNF-α and BSA decreases the peak potential separation value (ΔEp = Epa -Epc) as presented in Table 2 . The values are 0.764 for CD-PMMA/ITO and 0.448 for BSA/anti-TNF-α/CD-PMMA/ITO. This demonstrates a slow electron transfer process at the immunoelectrode and electrolyte interface.

The diffusion coefficient is an integral part of electrochemical reactions. It governs the diffusional transport of the redox species participating in the reaction. The value of D is calculated from the Randles- Sevcik equation which is
(1)
i p =
(
2.69
X
10 5
)
n
3 2
A
D
1 2
C
v
1 2
Where, I
p ; peak current (ipa/ipc) from CV at the scan rate of 0.05 V s-1, A; area of the working electrode, C is electrolyte concentration (5 mM) and N; no. of electrons participated in the redox reaction.

D is the rate of diffusion of analyte from solution to the electrode. The value of D was obtained as 1.97 × 10−4 for CD-PMMA/ITO electrode and 1.823 × 10−4 for BSA/anti-TNF-α/CD-PMMA/ITO immunoelectrode (Table 2). There is a marginal decrease in the value of D for these electrodes suggesting a slight reduction in electron diffusion to the immunoelectrode from the redox species in the electrolyte. This could be because of the blocking by BSA onto the electrode surface.

The I∗: surface concentration of ionic species (mol cm−2) was calculated using the Brown- Anson equation:
(2)
I p =
n 2
F 2
I ∗
A v
4 R T
Where T is the RT in kelvin, F is the Faraday constant and R is the gas constant (8.314 J mol−1 K−1).

The values of I∗ were obtained as: 2.022 × 10−2 for CD-PMMA/ITO electrode and 1.94 × 10−2 for BSA/anti-TNF-α/CD-PMMA/ITO immunoelectrode (Table 2). The slight change in the value of I∗ for BSA/anti-TNF-α/CD-PMMA/ITO immunoelectrode reveals the functionalization of immunoelectrode with BSA.

The parameter heterogeneous electron transfer rate constant (K s) reveals the change in the electron transfer kinetics at the interface of the electrolyte/electrode. The rate constant (K s) for each immunoelectrode was calculated using the Laviron model.
(3)
K s
=
m n F v
R T
.
Where symbols represent the same meaning as above.

The calculated values of Ks for CD-PMMA/ITO and BSA/anti-TNF-α/CD-PMMA/ITO obtained are 1.476 and 0.865 s−1, respectively. The high value of Ks for CD-PMMA/ITO represents that CD being rich in electroactive species contributes in the electron transfer process thereby enhancing the magnitude of current.

The analytical performance of the fabricated immunosensor was studied by amperometry in PBS 7.4 to detect different conditions of TNF-α using CD-PMMA/ITO electrode as the signal magnification platform towards the binding of anti-TNF-α to TNF-α. The detection of TNF-α was based on the degree of favoured electron transport by coupling of the antigen-antibody complex on the BSA/anti-TNF-α/CD-PMMA/ITO immunoelectrode. As shown in Fig. 5 (a), the amperometric signal increased with TNF-α concentration. The response of the immunosensor was found linear with the current values of the TNF-α concentration in the range of 0.01–100 pg mL−1 and a low LOD of 1.39 pg mL−1 (3σ/k). The equation of the calibration curve is given by:
(4) y = 18.139 + 0.075x
with an association coefficient of 0.998. These results suggest that the proposed immunosensor is capable of TNF-α detection.

The present immunosensor was compared with those reported in the literature as shown in Table 1. The proposed immunosensor detects TNF-α in a wide range as 0.05–160 pg mL−1 and a good lower detection limit (LOD) as 0.05 pg mL−1. The immunosensor has improved sensitivity over other sensors reported in the literature, which may be credited to the following considerations: firstly, the high surface to volume ratio of CDs helps in the higher immobilization of the anti-TNF-α through covalent bonding. Secondly, the remarkable electroconductivity of CDs increases the electron transfer speed.

In CD-PMMA composite, PMMA does not have any functional groups to link with the biomolecules [19,52]. To immobilize the biomolecules on the PMMA surface, the plasma treatment was used by which the reactive groups like –COOH, –NH2 was created on the polymer surface [19,53]. In the present case, the immobilization was easy without plasma treatment as the CDs already possess the –COOH, –NH2, –OH and so on, reactive groups on the surface which helps in the successful immobilization of the antibodies (Fig. 6 ). For further enhancement of the immobilization capability, the EDC-NHS treatment was also carried out. The activated antibodies were attached with the functional –NH2 groups present on CDs. The DPV peak current was decreased after the immobilization of the anti-TNF-α and BSA, due to the non-conducting nature of the biomolecules. When the analyte TNF-α was introduced into the buffer during response study, the current increased with concentration of the analyte.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Interference, reproducibility, repeatability, and stability studies
ce:section.ce:para: To illustrate the inter-assay obstruction of immunoelectrode from various serum components and other interleukin cancer biomarkers, an interference study was done (Fig. 7 a). The interferents were used like glucose, KCl, IL-8, MUC-16, CYFRA-21, and NaCl. The RSD values were observed as 3.45, 3.63, 3.16, 3.45, 1.79, and 1.75, respectively. To further elucidate the inter-and intra-assay, reliability and reproducibility of the proposed immunosensor, five different immunosensors currents were measured and the relative standard deviation (% RSD) was 1.26%, indicating good reproducibility of immunoelectrode (Fig. 7b). The repeatability was also appreciable (Fig. 7c) as there was no variation in electrode reading in multiple cycles. To verify the stability, immunosensors were stored at 4 °C for 5 weeks and every week under the same experimental conditions the current value was determined (Fig. 7d). These data showed that our proposed immunosensor has good stability for 4 weeks.

To validate the efficacy and the viability of the proposed immunosensor for the real sample application, blood samples of five cancer patients were tested. The samples were collected from the different patients with lesions or swelling in the mouth caused by tobacco consumption over several years. The concentration of the TNF-α in cancer patients was estimated using ELISA. The patients showed a wide variation in the level of TNF-α from 7.75 pg mL−1 to as high as 196.17 pg mL−1 (Fig. 5c). The higher levels of TNF-α were found in the patients with confirmed cancerous lesions in the mouth. The patient with a suspicious lesion was having the lowest TNF-α concentration which was not quantified by the ELISA kit as it was below the standard sample run in ELISA (the unknown concentration depends on the standard concentration run in ELISA. Here, we have run standard within the range of 7 pg mL−1 to 500 pg mL−1 since the normal level of TNF-α is 3 pg mL−1 in healthy individuals and up to 30 pg mL−1 in cancer patients). The ELISA readings further authenticated the relation between TNF-α and cancer. Different immunoelectrode were used to detect the cancerous patient sample under the same experimental conditions. The current values acquired by the standard and the one with serum sample were quite comparable with low RSD (0.95–9.22%) suggesting the immunosensor to be of sublime nature and had a good clinical analytical performance.

The POC application capability of the fabricated immunosensor was manifested here. Firstly, the immunosensor was based on highly reproducible CDs which can be synthesized quickly without any impediment. Further, these CDs were mixed with PMMA and drop cast on ITO electrodes which can be easily fabricated. Secondly, CD-PMMA/ITO electrode was used to study the change in the level of TNF-α to detect cancer. The immunoelectrode showed good LOD and high sensitivity of 5.56 pg mL−1 cm−2 over the others reported in the literature. Finally, the proposed immunosensor showed promising results and fast detection (20 min) over other methods as well as ELISA. Consequently, the fabricated immunosensor was very suitable for TNF-α detection in cancer patients.

In this article, an innovative electrochemical immunosensor platform based on CD-PMMA was successfully developed for the sensitive detection of TNF-α in cancer patient's serum samples. This is the first report of exploring the potential of CDs to be used as immunosensor for the early cancer diagnosis. A simple drop-casting method was employed to fabricate electrodes. PMMA was used as the matrix for CDs. The immunosensor was formed by immobilizing monoclonal anti-TNF-α antibody onto CD-PMMA/ITO electrodes. The CDs surface is rich in the carboxylic group and amine group which enables the formation of a covalent bond between Fc region of anti-TNF-α and CD surface. CD also provides more hydrophilicity to the electrodes making the immobilization more efficient. The TNF-α detection was based on the property of the immunocomplex formation through specific binding of anti-TNF-α and TNF-α which accelerates the electron transfer process and therefore increases the current response, proportional to the TNF-α concentration. The experimental linear range was from 0.01 to 160 pg mL−1 of TNF-α, with the LOD of 0.05 pg mL−1 corresponding correlation coefficient of 0.998 and improved sensitivity of 5.56 pg mL−1 cm−2. The proposed immunosensor was then applied to detect the TNF-α levels in the cancer patient's serum sample. The immunosensor showed promising results and faster detection (20 min) comparable with traditional ELISA method's results with acceptable relative error. On that account, it can be concluded that, the advancement of facile synthesis of CDs, easy fabrication, wide linear range, low LOD, high sensitivity and accuracy can be used to scale up for the detection of TNF-α in cancer patient's serum samples. Our developed immunosensor is useful for POC testing of tumor markers and therefore can be used for disease screening in remote areas also.

Smriti Sri: have formulated the experimental work, have carried out synthesis and experimental work, Writing review and editing. G.B.V.S. Lakshmi: have formulated the experimental work, have carried out synthesis and experimental work, Writing – review & editing, Supervision, have edited and supervised the manuscript preparation and experimental work. Payal Gulati: have carried out synthesis and experimental work. Deepika Chauhan: have carried out synthesis and experimental work. Alok Thakkar: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, have edited and supervised the manuscript preparation and experimental work. Pratima R. Solanki: have formulated the experimental work, Writing – review & editing, Supervision, have edited and supervised the manuscript preparation and experimental work.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Authors, Smriti Sri and Deepika Chauhan are thankful to UGC, New Delhi, India for providing fellowship in the form of SRF. GBVSL is thankful to DST, India funding through the women scientist project. PRS is thankful to DST, India for funding. This work was financially supported by the Department of Biotechnology (DBT); Indo-Russia project (DBT/IC-2/Indo-Russia/2017-19/02), ICMR project, and Department of Science and Technology (DST); BDTD project (TDP/BDTD/24/2019/General), Government of India.
